I found the NYSED 2026 Educator Guide for Grades 3-8 ELA tests, which includes information about the constructed response rubrics. Here's what I found:

## Overview of Constructed Response Questions

The 4-credit constructed-response questions are designed to assess Writing from Sources. In Grades 4–8, 4-credit constructed-response questions will require students to read and analyze paired texts.

## 2-Credit Constructed Response Rubric

Responses should typically require no more than approximately two or three complete sentences. A full-credit response is characterized by both a valid inference and sufficient textual support.

## 4-Credit Constructed Response Rubric

Many 4-credit constructed-response questions will ask students to express a position and support it with text-based details. The 4-credit constructed-response questions allow students to demonstrate their ability to write a coherent essay. Whether in response to an individual text or a paired selection, a student will be asked to synthesize, evaluate, and provide evidence for their thinking in a coherent and legible manner.

## Key Scoring Guidelines

If the prompt requires two texts and the student only references one text, the response can be scored no higher than a 2. If the student writes only a personal response and makes no reference to the text(s), the response can be scored no higher than a 1. Responses totally unrelated to the topic, illegible, or incoherent should be given a 0.

The official document with the complete rubrics is available at: https://www.nysed.gov/sites/default/files/programs/state-assessment/3-8-educator-guide-ela.pdf

103	Foreword
104	The information contained in this Educator Guide is designed to raise educator awareness of the structure of 
105	the New York State Grades 3–8 English Language Arts Tests aligned to the New York State Next Generation 
106	Learning Standards (https://www.nysed.gov/standards-instruction/english-language-arts).
107	The guide provides educators with pertinent information about the test development process, the learning 
108	standards that the tests are designed to measure, and the format of the testing sessions, which includes what 
109	types of questions will be asked and the average length of the testing sessions. Links to additional resources 
110	are provided to further enhance educators’ understanding of the structure of the English Language Arts tests. 
111	Educators are encouraged to review the guides prior to the test administration to gain familiarity with the 
112	test format. The information presented can also be used as a platform for educator discussion on how to best 
113	utilize student assessment results to guide future instruction.
114	The Elementary and Intermediate testing schedule for the spring administration can be found on
115	the Department’s website (https://www.nysed.gov/state-assessment/grades-3-8-test-schedules). Questions 
116	regarding the New York State Testing Program and test design may be addressed to the Office of State 
117	Assessment at emscassessinfo@nysed.gov. Questions regarding the New York State Learning Standards 
118	may be addressed to the Office of Standards and Instruction at P12StandardsInstruction@nysed.gov.
119	Grades 3–8 English Language Arts Educator Guide
120	2
121	New York State Grades 3–8 Testing Program 
122	Purpose of State Testing 
123	The federal Every Student Succeeds Act (2015) requires that states annually administer tests in English 
124	Language Arts (ELA) and Mathematics in Grades 3–8. The Grades 3–8 ELA and Mathematics NYS Testing 
125	Program has been designed to measure student knowledge and skills as defined by the grade-level New 
126	York Next Generation Learning Standards (NGLS) in ELA and Mathematics. The tests are designed to 
127	report student proficiency in one of four performance levels. Please refer to page 5 of this guide for further 
128	information regarding the Performance Level Descriptions.
129	New York State Educators Involvement in Test Development
130	While teachers have always been included in the Grades 3–8 test development process, the New York State 
131	Education Department (NYSED) continues to expand the number of opportunities for New York State 
132	educators to become involved. This includes participating in the development of all the test questions. New 
133	York State educators provide the critical input necessary to ensure that the tests are fair, valid, and appropriate 
134	for students through their participation in many test development activities. 
135	The test development process includes the development, review, and approval of test questions, construction 
136	of operational test forms, final approval of test forms prior to administration, and the development of

143	The Next Generation English Language Arts Learning Standards 
144	The New York State Next Generation English Language Arts Learning Standards define the knowledge, 
145	skills, and understandings that individuals can and do habitually demonstrate over time when exposed to 
146	high-quality instructional environments and learning experiences. The standards are organized into four 
147	overlapping strands, which together support the development of advanced literacy skills: Reading, Writing, 
148	Language, and Speaking and Listening. 
149	The information below is meant to provide the context and expectations to enable student success and inform 
150	teacher practice.
151	Lifelong Practices of Readers and Writers
152	The Lifelong Practices of Readers and Writers reflect the changing expectations for what it means to be 
153	literate today. Coursework and instruction based on the ELA Learning Standards should develop within the 
154	context of the Lifelong Practices of Readers and Writers. These practices are a context for the New York 
155	State ELA Learning Standards which, in turn, support these practices by specifying grade-level expectations 
156	for readers and writers.
157	Reading
158	Students in Grades 3–8 should experience a balance of literature and informational texts in the context 
159	of instruction designed to create opportunities for children to engage with a variety of topics and texts, 
160	and have discussions about texts that support language development and knowledge building. Creating this 
161	learning environment can take a variety of formats, including read-alouds, shared readings, paired readings, 
162	independent readings (beginning in Grade 4), and other learning activities that incorporate literacy materials, 
163	talking, and writing.
164	Writing
165	As students develop their writing skills, they will use a variety of strategies to plan, revise, and strengthen 
166	their writing as they work independently and collaboratively with adults and peers to produce texts and 
167	to learn about and develop oral language—written language and reading—writing connections. Students 
168	will write for multiple purposes (to entertain, to explain, to persuade) and learn about various tools (print 
169	and digital) to produce, share, and publish writing. In all writing tasks, students will learn to use and adjust 
170	language to best communicate ideas, content, and message. From Grade 3 through Grade 8, students will 
171	develop an understanding of the distinction between—and appropriate application of—conversational and 
172	academic language.
173	Language
174	To develop academic language and background knowledge, and to enrich personal language and expression, 
175	standards-based language instruction will enable students to increasingly demonstrate control of the 
176	conventions of academic language, develop their knowledge of language (understanding how language 
177	functions in different contexts, making effective choices for meaning and style, and comprehending more 
178	fully when reading or listening), and build skills relating to vocabulary acquisition and use (learning general 
179	academic and content specific words and phrases, clarifying the meaning of unknown or multiple meaning 
180	words and phrases, and demonstrating understanding of figurative language and nuances in meaning).
181	Grades 3–8 English Language Arts Educator Guide
182	4
183	Speaking and Listening
184	Language develops in the classroom if there is an extended back-and-forth process of interactions among 
185	students, organized around rich content and topics. Students will build their speaking and listening skills and 
186	knowledge by participating effectively in a range of conversations and collaborations with diverse partners; 
187	expressing ideas clearly and persuasively; integrating and evaluating information presented in diverse media 
188	and formats; evaluating points of view and reasoning; reasonably and clearly presenting information with 
189	supporting evidence; ensuring that organization and style are appropriate to task, purpose, and audience; 
190	making strategic use of media; adapting speech to context; and demonstrating command of academic English.
191	For more information about the Next Generation Learning Standards for English Language Arts 
192	& Literacy, please refer to the NYSED website (https://www.nysed.gov/standards-instruction/englishlanguage-arts).
193	Grades 3–8 English Language Arts Educator Guide

195	Performance Level Definitions
196	For each subject area, students perform along a continuum of the knowledge and skills necessary to meet the 
197	demands of the Learning Standards for English Language Arts and Mathematics. New York State assessments 
198	are designed to classify student performance into one of four levels based on the knowledge and skills the 
199	student has demonstrated. Due to the need to identify student proficiency, the state tests must provide students 
200	at each performance level opportunities to demonstrate their knowledge and skills in the NGLS. For this

200	at each performance level opportunities to demonstrate their knowledge and skills in the NGLS. For this 
201	reason, the Performance Level Descriptions play a central role in the test development process, specifically 
202	question writing.
203	These performance levels are defined as:
204	NYS Level 4
205	Students performing at this level excel in standards for their grade. They demonstrate knowledge, skills, and 
206	practices embodied by the Learning Standards that are considered more than sufficient for the expectations 
207	at this grade.
208	NYS Level 3
209	Students performing at this level are proficient in standards for their grade. They demonstrate knowledge, 
210	skills, and practices embodied by the Learning Standards that are considered sufficient for the expectations 
211	at this grade.
212	NYS Level 2
213	Students performing at this level are partially proficient in standards for their grade. They demonstrate 
214	knowledge, skills, and practices embodied by the Learning Standards that are considered partial but 
215	insufficient for the expectations at this grade. Students performing at Level 2 are considered on track to meet 
216	current New York high school graduation requirements but are not yet proficient in Learning Standards at 
217	this grade.
218	NYS Level 1
219	Students performing at this level are below proficient in standards for their grade. They may demonstrate 
220	limited knowledge, skills, and practices embodied by the Learning Standards that are considered insufficient
221	for the expectations at this grade.

232	using multiple-choice, 2-credit constructed-response, and 4-credit constructed-response questions. All 
233	questions will be based on close reading of informational and literary texts, including paired texts in 
234	Grades 4–8. All texts will be grade-level appropriate. The length of the texts on the Grades 3–8 English

251	Speaking and Listening
252	While Speaking and Listening Standards will NOT be assessed on the State test, they remain two very 
253	important components of critical building blocks in students’ ability to read and write at grade level. Speaking 
254	and Listening Standards provide the dialogic building blocks that directly support students in acquiring the 
255	necessary skills and knowledge.

257	texts they need in order to meet the rigors of what is required in writing. It is imperative that teachers 
258	continue to instruct and assess the Speaking and Listening Standards in the classroom. Instructional resources 
259	and examples of formative assessments for the Speaking and Listening Standards can be found in the

477	The R1 Standards form the heart of all text-based instruction. Therefore, in all grades, the R1 Standards 
478	provide a foundation for all questions on the tests, as all will require text-based responses. While not assessed 
479	directly in questions, R1 Standards are present on the test in the form of rigorous, worthwhile texts. Similarly, 
480	W1 Standards are present in all constructed-response questions, which will require students to support their 
481	claims (inferences) using clear reasons and relevant evidence.
482	Question Formats
483	The Grades 3–8 English Language Arts Tests contain multiple-choice (1-credit), 2-credit, and 4-credit 
484	constructed-response questions. For multiple-choice questions, students select the correct response from 
485	four answer choices. For 2-credit and 4-credit constructed-response questions, students write an answer to 
486	an open-ended question. 
487	Multiple-Choice Questions
488	Multiple-choice questions are designed to assess Reading and Language Standards. They will ask students to 
489	analyze different aspects of a given text, including central idea, style elements, character and plot development, 
490	and vocabulary. Almost all questions, including vocabulary questions, will only be answered correctly if the 
491	student comprehends and makes use of the whole passage. 
492	Multiple-choice questions will assess Reading and Language Standards in a range of ways. Some will ask 
493	students to analyze aspects of text or vocabulary. Many questions will require students to combine skills. 
494	For example, questions may ask students to identify a segment of text that best supports the central idea. To 
495	answer correctly, a student must first comprehend the central idea and then show understanding of how that 
496	idea is supported. Questions will require more than rote recall or identification. Students will also be required 
497	to negotiate plausible, text-based distractors.3 Each distractor will require students to comprehend the whole 
498	passage.
499	Constructed-Response Questions
500	As writers, students develop and apply a variety of distinct skills for a variety of purposes and in a variety 
501	of formats. The written communication of ideas necessitates executive functioning and planning, language 
502	knowledge and dexterity, creativity, and expression. Students write formally and informally, extensive pieces 
503	and abbreviated vignettes, for reflection as well as expression, for academic purposes, and for personal 
504	enjoyment. The expansive scope of the knowledge, skills, and understandings inherent in writing instruction 
505	far exceeds what can be assessed on a single test or through a single assignment. The constructed-response 
506	questions on these tests assess only a limited slice of these, as described in the question descriptions below
507	3
508	Adistractor is an incorrect response that may appear to be a plausible correct response to a student who has not mastered the skill 
509	or concept being assessed.
510	Grades 3–8 English Language Arts Educator Guide
511	14
512	and detailed in the corresponding rubrics, which provide a straightforward and detailed explanation of how 
513	student responses are assessed. For example, the rubric calls for the use of relevant evidence and wellchosen facts, criteria which are no more or less weighted than the ability to skillfully link ideas, having a 
514	clear and purposeful organization, or demonstrating insightful comprehension and analysis. The rubrics do 
515	not prioritize the length of the response or reward the accumulation of evidence beyond what is asked for 
516	by the question. Educators preparing students for this exam are encouraged to emphasize the quality of the 
517	constructed responses consistent with the rubric criteria and remind students that the length of their response 
518	does not necessarily improve the quality of their response. 
519	2-Credit Constructed-Response Questions
520	The 2-credit constructed-response questions are designed to assess Reading and Language Standards. These 
521	are single questions for which students use textual evidence to support their own answers to an inferential 
522	question. These questions ask the student to make an inference (a claim, position, or conclusion) based on 
523	their analysis of the passage, state it in their own words, and then provide two pieces of text-based evidence 
524	to support their answer.
525	The purpose of the 2-credit constructed-response questions is to assess a student’s ability to comprehend 
526	and analyze text. In responding to these questions, students will be expected to write in complete sentences. 
527	Responses with a valid inference and two supporting details from the associated text receive full credit. 
528	Responses should typically require no more than approximately two or three complete sentences. The rubric 
529	used to evaluate these types of responses is provided on page 15. It is important to note that students who 
530	answer the question only using details from the text will NOT receive full credit. A full-credit response is 
531	characterized by both a valid inference and sufficient textual support.
532	4-Credit Constructed-Response Questions
533	The 4-credit constructed-response questions are designed to assess Writing from Sources. In Grades 4–8, 
534	4-credit constructed-response questions will require students to read and analyze paired texts. Paired 
535	texts are related by theme, genre, tone, time period, or other characteristics. Many 4-credit constructedresponse questions will ask students to express a position and support it with text-based details. The 4-credit 
536	constructed-response questions allow students to demonstrate their ability to write a coherent essay using 
537	textual evidence to support their ideas. Whether in response to an individual text or paired texts, a student 
538	needs to synthesize, evaluate, and provide their thinking in a coherent and concise manner. As with 2-credit 
539	constructed-response questions, students are encouraged to answer in an efficient manner.

547	English Language Arts Rubrics
548	The Grades 3–8 English Language Arts Tests will be scored using the rubrics below. Both the English 
549	Language Arts 2-Credit and 4-Credit Constructed-Response Rubrics reflect the demands called for by the 
550	Next Generation Learning Standards.
551	2-Credit Constructed-Response Rubric
552	The 2-credit constructed-response questions will ask students to make a claim, take a position, or draw a 
553	conclusion, and then support it with details. As such, the 2-credit Constructed-Response Rubric focuses on 
554	both the inference and evidence a student provides. This structure allows students to have a wide latitude in 
555	responding to each prompt so long as their response is supported by the text.
556	Additionally, the expectation for all 2-credit responses will be complete, coherent sentences. By weaving 
557	these elements together, the questions, responses, and scores remain firmly focused on student reading ability.

587	4-Credit Constructed-Response Rubric
588	One must be able to write for a purpose using information from textual sources. The 4-credit constructedresponse questions on the English Language Arts Tests will ask students to analyze texts and address 
589	meaningful questions using strategic, textual details. Scores for 4-credit constructed-response questions will 
590	be based on four overarching criteria:
591	• Content and Analysis—the extent to which the essay conveys complex ideas and information clearly 
592	and accurately in order to support claims in an analysis of topics or texts
593	• Command of Evidence—the extent to which the essay presents evidence from the provided texts to 
594	support analysis and reflection
595	• Coherence, Organization, and Style—the extent to which the essay logically organizes complex 
596	ideas, concepts, and information using formal style and precise language
597	• Control of Conventions—the extent to which the essay demonstrates command of the conventions 
598	of standard English grammar, usage, capitalization, punctuation, and spelling

Grade 3 English Language Arts
Released Questionsfrom 2024 Exams
Background
As in past years, SED is releasing large portions of the 2024 NYS Grades 3–8 English Language Arts and Mathematics
test materialsfor review, discussion, and use.
For 2024, included in these released materials are at least 75 percent of the test questions that appeared on the
2024 tests (including all constructed-response questions) that counted toward students’ scores. Additionally, SED is
providing information about the released passages; the associated text complexity for each passage; and a map that
details what learning standards each released question measures and the correct response to each question. These
released materials will help students, families, educators, and the public better understand the tests and the New
York State Education Department’s expectations for students.
Understanding ELA Questions
Multiple-ChoiceQuestions
Multiple-choice questions are designed to assess the New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning Standards in
English Language Arts. These questions ask students to analyze different aspects of a given text, including central
idea, style elements, character and plot development, and vocabulary. Almost all questions, including vocabulary
questions, will be answered correctly only if the student comprehends and makes use of the whole passage.
For multiple-choice questions, students select the correct response from four answer choices. Multiple-choice
questions assess reading standards in a variety of ways. Some ask students to analyze aspects of text or vocabulary.
Many questions require students to combine skills. For example, questions may ask students to identify a segment of
text that best supports the central idea. To answer these questions correctly, a student must first comprehend the
central idea and then show understanding of how that idea is supported. Questions tend to require more than rote
recall or identification.
Two-Credit Constructed-ResponseQuestions
Two-credit constructed-response questions are designed to assess New York State P–12 Reading and Language
Standards. These are single questions in which a student uses textual evidence to support his or her answer to an
inferential question. These questions ask the student to make an inference (a claim, position, or conclusion) based on
their analysis of the passage, and then provide two pieces of text-based evidence to support their answer.
The purpose of the two-credit constructed-response questions is to assess a student’s ability to comprehend and
analyze text. In responding to these questions, students are expected to write in complete sentences. Responses
require no more than three complete sentences. The rubric used for evaluating two-credit constructed-response 
questions can be found in the grade-level Educator Guides at https://www.nysed.gov/state-assessment/
grades-3-8-ela-math-and-science-test-manuals.
New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning Standards Alignment
The alignment to the New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning Standards for English Language Arts is intended
to identify the analytic skills necessary to successfully answer each question. However, some questions measure
proficiencies described in multiple standards, including writing and additional reading and language standards. For
example, two-credit and four-credit constructed-response questions require students to first conduct the analyses
described in the mapped standard and then produce written responses that are rated based on writing
standards. To gain greater insight into the measurement focus for constructed-response questions, please refer to
the rubrics.
These Released Questions Do Not Comprise a “Mini Test”
To ensure it is possible to develop future tests, some content must remain secure. This document is not intended
to be representative of the entire test, to show how operational tests look, or to provide information about how
teachers should administer the test; rather, its purpose is to provide an overview of how the test reflects the demands
of the New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning Standards.
The released questions do not represent the full spectrum of the standards assessed on the State tests, nor do they
represent the full spectrum of how the standards should be taught and assessed in the classroom. It should not be
assumed that a particular standard will be measured by an identical question in future assessments.
2024 Grade 3 ELA Test Text Complexity Metrics for
Released Questions 
Selecting high-quality, grade-appropriate passages requires both objective text 
complexity metrics and expert judgment. For the Grades 3–8 assessments based on the 
New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning Standards for English Language Arts, both 
quantitative and qualitative rubrics are used to determine the complexity of the texts and 
their appropriate placement within a grade-level ELA exam.
Quantitative measures of text complexity are used to measure aspects of text complexity 
that are difficult for a human reader to evaluate when examining a text. These aspects 
include word frequency, word length, sentence length, and text cohesion. These aspects 
are efficiently measured by computer programs. While quantitative text complexity 
metrics are a helpful start, they are not definitive.
Qualitative measures are a crucial complement to quantitative measures. Using 
qualitative measures of text complexity involves making an informed decision about the 
difficulty of a text in terms of one or more factors discernible to a human reader applying 
trained judgment to the task. To qualitatively determine the complexity of a text, NYS 
educators use a rubric composed of five factors; four of these factors are required and 
one factor is optional. The required criteria are: meaning, text structure, language 
features, and knowledge demands. The optional factor, graphics, is used only if a graphic 
appears in the text.
To make the final determination as to whether a text is at grade-level and thus 
appropriate to be included on a Grades 3–8 assessment, New York State uses a two-step 
review process, which is an industry best-practice. First, all prospective passages undergo 
quantitative text complexity analysis using three text complexity measures. If at least two 
of the three measures suggest that the passage is grade-appropriate, the passage then 
moves to the second step, which is the qualitative review using the text-complexity 
rubrics. Only passages that are determined appropriate by at least two of three 
quantitative measures of complexity and are determined appropriate by the qualitative 
measure of complexity are deemed appropriate for use on the exam.

TIPS FOR TAKING THE TEST
Here are some ideas to help you do your best:
• Read the whole passage before you answer the questions. Most questions will only make 
sense after you read the whole passage.
• You might need to read the passage more than once to answer a question. 
• Read each question carefully. Take your time.
• A question may include a quote from a passage. You might need to review both the
quote and the whole passage to answer the question.
When you write your answers
• make sure to answer the whole question; 
• use examples or details from the text; 
• write in complete sentences; and
• use correct spelling, grammar, capitalization, and punctuation.

---

# NYSED 2026 Educator Guide: Grades 3-8 ELA Tests -- Complete Research Report

**Source Document**: *New York State Testing Program: Educator Guide to the Grades 3-8 English Language Arts Tests* (Updated November 2025, Copyright 2026)
**Publisher**: New York State Education Department (NYSED), developed under contract by NWEA (a division of HMH)

---

## 1. EXACT RUBRIC FOR 2-CREDIT CONSTRUCTED-RESPONSE QUESTIONS (All Grades Including Grade 3)

The 2-credit rubric is **universal across Grades 3-8** (Grade 3 does NOT have 4-credit questions; those begin at Grade 4).

### 2-Credit Constructed-Response Rubric

**Score: 2 Credits** -- The features of a 2-credit response are:
- Valid inferences and/or claims from the text where required by the prompt
- Evidence of analysis of the text where required by the prompt
- Relevant facts, definitions, concrete details, and/or other information from the text to develop response according to the requirements of the prompt
- Sufficient number of facts, definitions, concrete details, and/or other information from the text as required by the prompt
- Complete sentences where errors do not impact readability

**Score: 1 Credit** -- The features of a 1-credit response are:
- A mostly literal recounting of events or details from the text as required by the prompt
- Some relevant facts, definitions, concrete details, and/or other information from the text to develop response according to the requirements of the prompt
- Incomplete sentences or bullets

**Score: 0 Credits** -- The features of a 0-credit response are:
- A response that does not address any of the requirements of the prompt or is totally inaccurate
- A response that is not written in English
- A response that is unintelligible or indecipherable

**Special Scoring Rules:**
- If the prompt requires two texts and the student only references one text, the response can be scored no higher than a 1.
- Condition Code A is applied whenever a student who is present for a test session leaves an entire constructed-response question completely blank (no response attempted).

### Key Guidance from NYSED on 2-Credit Responses:
- Students must make an **inference** (a claim, position, or conclusion) based on their analysis of the passage, state it in their own words, and then provide **two pieces of text-based evidence** to support their answer.
- Responses should typically require **no more than approximately two or three complete sentences**.
- Students who answer the question **only using details from the text** (without making an inference) will NOT receive full credit.
- A full-credit response is characterized by **both a valid inference AND sufficient textual support**.
- The rubric does NOT prioritize length or reward accumulation of evidence beyond what is asked.

---

## 2. SAMPLE/EXAMPLE QUESTIONS WITH CORRECT FORMAT

### 2-Credit Constructed-Response Question Examples (Grade 3, from 2024 Released Test)

**Format**: Each question begins with "This question is worth 2 credits." and asks the student to "Use two details from the passage/story to support your response."

**Example Question 24 (Session 1, Literary Text):**
> "In 'Excerpt from The Little Red Lighthouse and the Great Gray Bridge,' why does the little red lighthouse feel proud? Use two details from the story to support your response."
- Standard: NY-3.R.3 (Reading)
- Subscore: Writing to Sources
- Average points earned: 1.03 out of 2 (P-value: 0.51)

**Example Question 25 (Session 1, Literary Text):**
> "What theme is best supported by paragraphs 17 through 25 of 'Excerpt from The Little Red Lighthouse and the Great Gray Bridge'? Use two details from the story to support your response."
- Standard: NY-3.R.2 (Reading)
- Subscore: Writing to Sources
- Average points earned: 0.96 out of 2 (P-value: 0.48)

**Example Question 32 (Session 2, Informational Text):**
> "In 'Excerpt from Hide with the Ladybugs,' how does paragraph 5 connect to paragraph 6? Use two details from the passage to support your response."
- Standard: NY-3.R.3
- Average points earned: 1.24 out of 2 (P-value: 0.62)

**Example Question 33 (Session 2, Informational Text):**
> "The cold can be dangerous for animals, so they find ways to stay safe in bad weather. How does 'Excerpt from Hide with the Ladybugs' develop this idea? Use two details from the passage to support your response."
- Standard: NY-3.R.9
- Average points earned: 1.00 out of 2 (P-value: 0.50)

**Example Question 34 (Session 2, Informational with Diagram):**
> "How does the diagram 'A Ladybug's Body' better help the reader's understanding of the passage? Use two details from the passage to support your response."
- Standard: NY-3.R.7
- Average points earned: 1.01 out of 2 (P-value: 0.51)

### Guidance from Appendix A (2018 Sample):
The guide references a four-sentence response to a Grade 3 question assessing Reading Standard 5 from the 2018 released materials that received full credit. Students are directed to concise, efficient responses.

## 3. HOW MULTIPLE-CHOICE QUESTIONS ARE STRUCTURED

### Stem Structure:
- Each MC question has **one stem** (the question) and **four answer choices** (A, B, C, D).
- Students select **one correct response**.
- Each MC question is worth **1 credit**.

### Question Design Principles:
- Questions assess **Reading and Language Standards**.
- They ask students to analyze: **central idea, style elements, character and plot development, and vocabulary**.
- Almost all questions, including vocabulary questions, will only be answered correctly if the student **comprehends and makes use of the whole passage**.
- Questions require **more than rote recall or identification**.

### Distractor Design:
The guide explicitly states: "Students will also be required to negotiate **plausible, text-based distractors**." Each distractor is defined as "an incorrect response that may appear to be a plausible correct response to a student who has not mastered the skill or concept being assessed." Each distractor requires students to comprehend the whole passage.

### Actual Grade 3 MC Question Formats (from 2024 released items):

**Vocabulary in context:**
> "What does the word 'muses' mean as it is used in paragraph 2?"
> A) feels strange  B) becomes amazed  C) thinks deeply  D) feels upset

**Phrase/figurative language:**
> "The phrase 'change before your very eyes' in paragraph 8 suggests that clouds..."
> A) can only be seen up close  B) can look strange to others  C) can make it difficult for you to see  D) can become different while you watch

**Author's claim/central idea:**
> "What claim does the author make about cumulus clouds?"

**Text structure/organization:**
> "In which section of the passage can the reader find information about how clouds are made?"

**Cause and effect:**
> "Which sentence from the passage shows a cause-and-effect relationship?"

**Author's perspective:**
> "Which detail best shows how the author feels about clouds?"

**Summary:**
> "Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the passage?"

**Character analysis:**
> "Which sentence best explains why Molly acts this way?" (with embedded quote from passage)

**Theme:**
> "Which sentence best shows a theme of the story?"

**Point of view:**
> "In paragraph 3 of the story, whose thoughts are being shown?"

**Character change:**
> "Which sentence best shows how Molly's feelings about Sam change in the story?"

**Text evidence (connecting outside info to text):**
> "Lighthouses are important because they guide boats... Which detail best shows how this idea connects to the events in the story?"

### Key Design Principle:
The R1 Standards (citing textual evidence) provide a foundation for ALL questions on the tests, as all require text-based responses. W1 Standards are present in all constructed-response questions, requiring students to support their claims using clear reasons and relevant evidence.

## 5. PASSAGE COMPLEXITY REQUIREMENTS FOR GRADE 3

### Word Count:
**Grade 3 passages: 525-625 words** (ranges may vary slightly to create a more complete excerpt that aids student comprehension)

### Quantitative Text Complexity Bands (Grade 2-3 Band):

| Measure | Range for 2nd-3rd Grade Band |
|---------|------------------------------|
| ATOS | 2.75 - 5.14 |
| Degrees of Reading Power | 42 - 54 |
| Flesch-Kincaid | 1.98 - 5.34 |
| The Lexile Framework | 420 - 820 |
| Reading Maturity | 3.53 - 6.13 |
| SourceRater | 0.05 - 2.48 |

### Actual 2024 Grade 3 Passage Metrics:

| Passage Title | Word Count | Lexile | Flesch-Kincaid | ATOS | Qualitative Review |
|---------------|-----------|--------|----------------|------|-------------------|
| Excerpt from Crazy About Clouds | 509 | 550 | 2.4 | 3.37 | Appropriate |
| Excerpt from The Little Red Lighthouse and the Great Gray Bridge | 618 | 530 | 2.3 | 3.8 | Appropriate |
| Excerpt from Apple Day | 528 | 580 | 3.2 | 4.27 | Appropriate |
| Excerpt from Hide with the Ladybugs | 580 | 550 | 3.3 | 3.13 | Appropriate |

### Two-Step Complexity Review Process:
1. **Quantitative analysis** using three text complexity measures -- at least 2 of 3 must indicate grade-appropriateness
2. **Qualitative review** using rubrics with five factors: **meaning, text structure, language features, knowledge demands** (required), and **graphics** (optional, used only if a graphic appears)

### Qualitative Review Criteria:
- Meaning
- Text structure
- Language features
- Knowledge demands
- Graphics (optional -- only when graphic is present)

## 6. SAMPLE PASSAGES AND QUESTION STEMS FROM THE GUIDE

### Grade 3 Test Design Summary:

| | Session 1 | Session 2 | Total |
|---|-----------|-----------|-------|
| Passages | 4 | 2 | 6 |
| Multiple-Choice Questions | 23 | 6 | 29 |
| 2-Credit CR Questions | 2 | 3 | 5 |
| 4-Credit CR Questions | 0 | 0 | **0** |
| Literary Passages | 3-4 total | | |
| Informational Passages | 2-3 total | | |

**Important**: Grade 3 has NO 4-credit constructed-response questions. Those begin in Grade 4.

### Session Structure:
- **Session 1**: 4 reading passages -- two with MC only, two with MC + 2-credit CR. Average time: 60-70 minutes.
- **Session 2**: 2 passages with MC or 2-credit CR questions. Average time: 70-80 minutes.
- No time limits; students get as much time as needed within the regular school day.

### Full Sample Passages from 2024 Released Items:

**Passage 1 (Informational)**: "Excerpt from Crazy About Clouds" by Rena Korb -- 509 words, Lexile 550. Covers cloud formation, types of clouds (stratus, cumulus, cirrus, cumulonimbus), weather prediction. Organized with section headings: "Tiny, Floating Drops," "Changing Clouds," "High Clouds, Low Clouds," "Types of Clouds," "Study the Clouds."

**Passage 2 (Literary)**: "Excerpt from The Little Red Lighthouse and the Great Gray Bridge" by Hildegarde H. Swift and Lynd Ward -- 618 words, Lexile 530. Classic story about a lighthouse on the Hudson River that feels insignificant when a great bridge is built next to it, but learns it is still needed.

**Passage 3 (Literary)**: "Excerpt from Apple Day" by Kimberly Long Cockroft -- 528 words, Lexile 580. Story about a girl named Molly making applesauce with her family, learning that her cousin Sam's unexpected contribution of tiny tart apples improves the recipe.

**Passage 4 (Informational)**: "Excerpt from Hide with the Ladybugs" by Karen Latchana Kenney -- 580 words, Lexile 550. Informational text about ladybug life cycle, body structure, habitat, and behavior. Organized with section headings: "Lovely Ladybug," "Growing Ladybugs," "Flying and Feeling," "Helpful Bugs," "Staying Safe," "New Lives."

### Common Question Stem Patterns for Grade 3:

**For MC questions:**
- "What does the word [X] mean as it is used in paragraph [N]?"
- "The phrase [X] in paragraph [N] suggests that..."
- "Which sentence from the passage shows a [cause-and-effect / compare-contrast] relationship?"
- "Which detail best shows how the author feels about [topic]?"
- "Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the passage?"
- "Which event would be most important to include in a summary of the story?"
- "How are the details in paragraphs [X] through [Y] important to the story?"
- "How does [event] affect [character]?"
- "Which sentence best shows a theme of the story?"
- "Which sentence best shows how [character's] feelings about [character] change in the story?"
- "In paragraph [N], whose thoughts are being shown?"
- "[Character] believes that [character]..."
- "Which quote from the story best shows that [character] has learned a lesson?"
- "Read these sentences from paragraph [N]. [Quote.] Which sentence best explains why [character] acts this way?"

**For 2-Credit CR questions:**
- "In [passage title], why does [character] feel [emotion]? Use two details from the story to support your response."
- "What theme is best supported by paragraphs [X] through [Y] of [passage title]? Use two details from the story to support your response."
- "In [passage title], how does paragraph [X] connect to paragraph [Y]? Use two details from the passage to support your response."
- "[General statement about a concept.] How does [passage title] develop this idea? Use two details from the passage to support your response."
- "How does the [diagram/illustration] [title] better help the reader's understanding of the passage? Use two details from the passage to support your response."
